responseSet.title: Further Adventures with Adolf Grünbaum: Response to Grünbaum
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Further Adventures with Adolf Grünbaum

Response to Grünbaum

Joseph Schwartz PHD

Joseph Schwartz

2 Lancaster Drive
London NW3 4HA
England
jadamschw@aol.com

Joseph Schwartz   Centre for Attachment-Based Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy, London, England.

On habermas and gadamer's important argument that psycho-analysis is fundamentally different from physics because the phenomena of physics are ahistorical and context independent, I emphasize that they are entirely correct. Physics deals with timeless forms of matter that appeared early in the evolution of the universe. As opposed to the phenomena of biology, the phenomena of physics are universal, independent of their location in space and time (context). One of the most important properties of the electron is that each electron is indistinguishable from all other electrons in the universe—a property that has as one consequence the periodic table of the chemical elements. The timeless universality of inanimate matter allows the construction of the great general laws of nature, of which physics can be justifiably proud.

As for Grünbaum's presentation of the physics of the so-called complete electromagnetic field and the hysteresis loop of a permanent magnet as counterexamples, I can in this space only reiterate the fact that there is no cumulative history in these systems as there is with DNA in its cellular environment. At any single point in time and space, the electromagnetic field created by a moving charge depends only on the position, velocity, and acceleration of the electric charge at a single previous time. Grünbaum's mistake is to assign historical significance to the fact that a specification of the electromagnetic field at every point in space at a given time—the so-called complete field—requires a knowledge of the past trajectory of the charge. At no point in space-time is the electric field cumulative.

343

[[RunningHead]]

Normalizing Citizenship, Forgetting Difference

Ann Pellegrini PHD

Ann Pellegrini

3009 Broadway
New York, NY 10027
ap168@columbia.edu

Ann Pellegrini   Ann Pellegrini, Ph.D. is an Associate Professor of Women's Studies at Barnard College, Columbia University. She is the author of Performance Anxieties: Staging Psychoanalysis, Staging Race (Routledge, 1997) and is completing a new book, Excess.

The american dream in black and white: the clarence thomas hearings by Jane Flax (Ithaca, NY, Cornell University Press, 1998, 170 pp.)

In this brief but suggestive new book, jane flax, a professor of political science at Howard University and practicing psychotherapist, asks why “the American political system [has] been unable to eliminate…inequalities” of race and gender (pp. 1-2). She responds with a feminist critique of liberalism's and America's most cherished assumptions about fairness, the heroic (and heroically abstract) individual, the “problem” of difference. Although the main points of Flax's arguments will not be news to most readers versed in contemporary feminist and critical theories, her choice of method is refreshing. She focuses her critique through a case study of the Clarence Thomas hearings of September and October 1991 and uses the hearings to expose the American race/gender system and its effects and persistent inequalities. As I discuss here, her method is not uniformly successful. However, when it works, her case study method animates her theoretical points and grounds her suggestion that race/gender domination, far from representing historical anomalies, is an ongoing and, even more disturbingly, constitutive feature of the American dream.

Throughout The American Dream in Black and White, Flax writes race/gender as one word.

701

responseSet.title: How We Institute Our Psychoanalytic Education: Reply to Commentaries
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

How We Institute Our Psychoanalytic Education

Reply to Commentaries

Randall Sorenson PHD Psy.D.

Randall Sorenson

100 North Hill Avenue, Suite 206
Pasadena, CA 91106
randysorenson@mac.com

Randall Sorenson  

I appreciate the thoughtful and differing commentaries by Drs. Bass, Druck, and Eisold. The points on which they differ can be viewed as helpful examples of the various ways that nearly all analysts will likely respond to the issues I raise about structuring our associations to facilitate psychoanalytic education. Because the argument in my essay progressed across three points (the idealization of science, imagining institutes as religious denominations, structuring psychoanalytic education), I return to each of these now as a way to continue the dialogue that the commentaries invite.

Idealization of Science

Philosopher Nancey Murphy (1990, 1997) has noted how the beginning and ending of the modern period—which in philosophy lasted from roughly 1650 until 1950—punctuate two turning points in epistemology. The first, which inaugurated modernity in the mid-17th century, entailed a rejection of medieval notions authority, whether feudal or ecclesiastical, in favor of a foundationalism that sought to reconstruct knowledge so as to establish a secure foundation from which to know everything else. The second turn, which occurred in the middle of the 20th century and is still in progress, involves a rejection of modernity's foundationalism in favor of a more holist epistemology.

I am grateful to James W. Jones, Ph.D., Psy.D. and Gary R. Sattler, Dr. theol., Psy.D., who offered insightful comments on an earlier version of this reply.

917

responseSet.title: Power, Puritanism, and Promiscuity: Pursuit of a President
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Power, Puritanism, and Promiscuity

Pursuit of a President

Dick Blackwell M. Inst. GA

Dick Blackwell

1 Daleham Gardens
London NW3 5BY
England
Iga@igalondon.org.uk

Dick Blackwell   Chair, Institute of Group Analysis (IGA), London, and is in private practice, London, as Group Analyst, Family Therapist, and Organization Consultant.

Tempting though it is to plunge straight in, a few preliminary remarks are in order, particularly when one is writing from the other side of the Atlantic.

Group analysis brings together psychology and sociology, psychoanalysis and society, the personal and the political. However, it does not do this as a completed theory that can be used to produce explanations of various individual or social actions. It is instead a process of analysis, which is never completed. Each explanation adds to a new whole which can then be further analyzed. Students of Hegel will immediately recognize the process.

Moreover, it is analysis of the group by the group, including the conductor. So, what I have to say about the Clinton affair is merely one contribution to the debate, which is in itself open to be interpreted and analyzed. It needs also to be understood as an English point of view.

Group analysis is particularly concerned with communication, so we need to look very hard at the communication through which we are informed, or misinformed, about this whole business. In an analytic group, we hear directly what others say, and we see them saying it. We know them directly, and we have time to get to know them. But a public figure is very much a persona. A persona, moreover, that is significantly constructed by her or his own public relations machine and the media on which we are all to some extent dependent for our information about what is going on in the world beyond our direct experience. This is of particular importance in relation to Clinton because there seems to be a growing gap between (a)

<autaff><instit>Charles University</instit><addr><ln>2nd Medical Faculty</ln><ln>17000 Prague 7, Korunovacn\'ed 18, Czech Republic</ln><ln>msebek@terminal.cz</ln></addr></autaff></artauth></artinfo>
responseSet.title: The Clinton Phenomenon
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Clinton Phenomenon

Michael Šebek CSC

Michael Šebek

2nd Medical Faculty
17000 Prague 7, Korunovacn\'ed 18, Czech Republic
msebek@terminal.cz

Michael Šebek   President, Czech Psychoanalytical Provisional Society, and Associate Professor of Medical Psychology and Medical Ethics, Charles University, 2nd Medical Faculty.

The clinton phenomenon creates conflict in those who observe it or engage in it. At first, one needs to overcome some cultural barrier. From the Czech perspective, it seems to be strange to set up a persecuting institution against the president. Czechs are not very eager to publicize the private sexual matters of representatives of the state. For instance, today it is known that the first president of Czechoslovakia, T. G. Masaryk, had mistresses until his very old age. These facts were and still are hidden with help of archives and their directors. The uncomfortable feelings that would spread if this were to be uncovered are part of conventional morality. Therefore, Clinton's case was observed in the Czech Republic with restraint. Although the attraction of uncovering primal scenes seems to be natural, some sublimated versions of this attraction into ordinary curiosity regulated by cultural values are generally preferred, and even more common. But the border between “perverse” and “normal” is not today so sharply marked.

Clinton is not an analytic case; therefore, he can be assessed only as “the Clinton phenomenon,” and his self-destructive behaviors belong to some other realm. The discovery and publication of his sexual adventures reveal him as an ordinary man, but at the same time the question arises about what we consciously and unconsciously expect from a powerful authority in the highest position. In ancient times, kings were close to gods; they were idealized as protectors and feared as persecutors. Some strong parental roles were delegated to them from those below. In democracy, all “kings” are far from gods, and they are not felt to be almighty, but still some parental protective and persecuting functions are projected onto them, together with some idealization that also involves desexualization.

315

responseSet.documentRef: Frankel, J. (2000) Who to Be or Not to Be?: Commentary on Papers on President Clinton's Impeachment. Psychoanalytic Dialogues 10:327-334

responseSet.documentInfoXML: 2000102327-334Who to Be or Not to Be?Commentary on Papers on President Clinton's ImpeachmentJay Frankel Co-Director, Manhattan Institute for Psychoanalysis; Supervisor, New York University Postdoctoral Program in Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis; and Associate Editor of Psychoanalytic Dialogues. 290 West 12th Street, 6BNew York, NY 10014Drjfrankel@aol.com
responseSet.title: Who to Be or Not to Be?: Commentary on Papers on President Clinton's Impeachment
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Who to Be or Not to Be?

Commentary on Papers on President Clinton's Impeachment

Jay Frankel PHD

Jay Frankel

290 West 12th Street, 6B
New York, NY 10014
Drjfrankel@aol.com

Jay Frankel   Co-Director, Manhattan Institute for Psychoanalysis; Supervisor, New York University Postdoctoral Program in Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis; and Associate Editor of Psychoanalytic Dialogues.

We have been treated to a panel of brief, interesting, and very different papers, all taking as a starting point the events surrounding the impeachment of President Clinton. These papers take off in diverging directions, using these events to explore the dynamics of hypocrisy (Rangell), particular varieties of individual pathology (Bloland, Orfanos), social pathology (Chasseguet-Smirgel, Orfanos), the nature of leadership and the relation of leaders to society (Blackwell, Samuels, Šebek, Žižek), and the nature and effects of power (Berenstein, Bloland). We owe the president thanks for serving, if only unwittingly and unhappily, as a foil for these various explorations.

Here I shall describe how, despite the different directions in which these papers go, there may be an underlying set of concerns that unifies them—a set of concerns that will force us to consider how we expect ourselves and our fellows to behave in our dealings with other people. First, though, I take these papers on their own terms. Bloland, and to a lesser extent Orfanos, takes a diagnostic approach, seeing Clinton as an example of a person with a particular kind of individual pathology—the pathology inherent in great ambition. Along similar lines, though Berenstein does not diagnose Clinton, he discusses how power can corrupt human relationships, and he makes the extraordinary observation that often someone who exercises power does not realize he or she is doing so.

The authors who diagnose Clinton take a risk, speculating beyond what they know about him. As Berenstein, Blackwell, and Šebek remind us, Clinton is not our patient; we know him only as a persona.

327

responseSet.title: Jung's Contribution to Psychoanalytic Thought
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Jung's Contribution to Psychoanalytic Thought

Donald Kalsched PHD

Donald Kalsched

23 Parkway Street, #3A
Katonah, NY 10536
donedkal@aol.com

Donald Kalsched   Clinical Psychologist and Jungian Psychoanalyst who practices in New York City and Katonah, New York He is Faculty and Supervisor, New York Jung Institute and Faculty Inter-regional Society of Jungian Analysts; Member, Division 39 of the American Psychological Association; and Dean, Jungian Studies Specialty, Westchester Institute for Training in Psychoanalysis and Psychotherapy, Mt. Kisco, New York.

Jung's Most Significant Clinical Contributions

Jung's most significant clinical contributions to analytic practice are:

His emphasis on the primacy of affect as the central organizing principle of psychic life—instead of the drives—and how this leads to an understanding of the psyche's inherent dissociability.

His insistence that psychoanalysis was best conducted in a “bipersonal field” in which both partners sit face-to-face and mutually influence the result.

His understanding of dreams and the symbolic expressions of fantasy life as images that give form to unconscious affects and hence are agents of evolving consciousness, not “compromise formations.” This led Jung to use various nonverbal techniques within the analytic situation, including drawing, painting, and active imagination.

His structural model (archetypes) of the psyche, which includes a “collective” or “psychoid” layer in which events occur that are not just psychological but psysical as well (echoing recent discoveries in both psychosomatics and atomic physics). This “magical” or “mystical” realm is the basis for religious experience and for synchronicity and other noncausal connective events otherwise known as “transgressive” or “paranormal.

473

responseSet.authorCitation: Gerhardt, J., Sweetnam, A. & Borton, L.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Intersubjective Turn in Psychoanalysis

A Comparison of Contemporary Theorists: Part 1: Jessica Benjamin

Julie Gerhardt PHD , Annie Sweetnam PHD and Leeann Borton MA

Leeann Borton

1453 Mission Street
San Francisco, CA 94103
gerhardtjulie@hotmail.com

1947 Divisadero St. Suite 5
San Francisco, CA 94115
asweetnm@aol.com

1453 Mission Street
San Francisco, CA 94103
lborton@earthlink.net

Leeann Borton   Leeann Borton, M.A. is a doctoral candidate at the California Institute of Integral Studies. She is a psychological assistant in San Francisco.

This paper on Benjamin is part 1 of a series presenting the work of three contemporary theorists whose ideas are associated with the intersubjective turn in psychoanalysis. Part 2, on Bollas, and part 3, on Ehrenberg, will appear in subsequent issues of Psychoanalytic Dialogues. Although we have made a minimal attempt to critically review the different theories, we have allowed ourselves the fiction of trying to produce a representational text in which the different arguments of the different theorists are presented, more or less, in their own terms. While this version of textual production on our part may be troubling, as it obscures as much as it reveals, insofar as our own position is never quite

We wish to thank Victor Bonfilio, Sam Gerson, and Charles Spezzano for comments on various parts of this paper. Needless to say, we are responsible for all its faults and foibles.

For the purposes of clarity and stylistic consistency it was decided that Gerhardt's academic writing style fits the needs of this paper.

5

declared, our intent is to try to minimize our own mediating voice and focus on the different theorists in their own right by giving clinical examples to demonstrate their claims. The irony, even folly, of attempting to eliminate our own presence from this series on intersubjectivity is not lost on us—and neither is our plea for special circumstances. However, given the growing interest in the intersubjective turn, in order that it not be construed as privileging the emotional authenticity of the two-person exchange, we believe it is absolutely essential to understand the theorists' self-articulated arguments and to keep alive their differences rather than to assimilate the intersubjective perspective as a unified or hegemonic approach. It is in the spirit of keeping alive these differences—itself a crucial commitment of the intersubjective approach—that our somewhat exegetical text should be understood.

responseSet.title: Dreams: Commentary of Paper by Hazel Ipp
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Dreams

Commentary of Paper by Hazel Ipp

Steven Ellman PHD

Steven Ellman

140 Riverside Drive
New York, NY 10024
cellman 174@aol.com

Steven Ellman   Professor of Psychology, Doctoral Program in Clinic Psychology, City University of New York; Training Analyst (Fellow), Institute of Psychoanalytic Training and Research (IPTAR); and Faculty Member and Supervisor, Postdoctoral Program in Psychoanalysis, New York University. He is the author or coauthor of several books.

It is somewhat awkward to admit that, although i have spent a reasonable amount of my career involved in dream research (Ellman and Antrobus, 1991), I have never written a clinical paper on dreams. This undoubtedly has to do with a conflict that is a constant background presence in my clinical work—whether I am interested in the dream or the dreamer. In other words, is my interest in dreams moving the patient to dream in particular ways to satisfy the demand characteristics of my analytic stance? Of course, Freud faced this question when he hypothesized that dreams were the royal road to the unconscious. Subsequent clinical experience led him to realize that royal roads were rapidly proving to be dead ends in Europe and in psychoanalysis. It was his intellectual discovery of the importance of transference (and, implicitly, countertransference) that influenced him to abandon his reliance on dream interpretation and state that, although he did not doubt the importance of the dream, he realized that to rely fully on dream interpretation will put a clinician “into conflict with the most immediate aims of the treatment” (Freud, 1911, quoted in Ellman, 1991, p. 110.

Theoretical Statement

143

<autaff><addr><ln>Republica de la India 2921, Apt. 9</ln><ln>(1425) Buenos Aires</ln><ln>Argentina</ln><ln>Email: iberens@intramed.net.ar</ln></addr></autaff></artauth></artinfo>
responseSet.title: The Clinton Impeachment: Sexuality and Power Need Different Psychoanalytic Theories
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Clinton Impeachment

Sexuality and Power Need Different Psychoanalytic Theories

Isidoro Berenstein MD

Isidoro Berenstein

Republica de la India 2921, Apt. 9
(1425) Buenos Aires
Argentina
Email: iberens@intramed.net.ar

Isidoro Berenstein   Training and Supervising Analyst, Buenos Aires Psychoanalytic Association; winner of the Mary Sigourney Award 1993 for outstanding contribution to psychoanalysis; and Scientific Chair of the Department of Family Relations, Argentine Group Psychology and Psychotherapy Association. The editors thank Ernesto Mujica, Ph.D. for additional assistance in translation.

Psychoanalysts obtain the empirical basis for interpretation from the patient's material in the course of the analytic session. That is the arena for observing and analyzing the clinical facts. President Clinton is not our patient, but he set in motion a number of events that reached us as social facts. I begin with the Clinton case in order to explore the structure of power in relationships. I suggest that we live simultaneously in three different worlds that produce three kinds of facts—individual, linking, and social. Individual facts are those that we arbitrarily say happen inside the subject, in the inner world. Linking facts are those that happen in the space “between” two or more subjects, and within this category kinship relationships should be differentiated. What is special about these relationships is that they take place with a specific, differentiated other. I refer to the “other” as a subject of desire that exceeds the internal object that is projected onto it. In this sense, it is not simply an object of drive derivative or love object of the ego. Social facts are those that reach us from the social milieu formed by the undifferentiated others, defensively personified in particular characters who represent the world of politics, religion, finance, social class. These three worlds leave marks on the self that may or may not become unconscious inscriptions. It is desirable to consider that each mark has a particular and different origin. Only a part of the meaning of each mark is liable to be transferred from one world to the other. The clinical material of an individual patient, of a couple, or of a family as a patient does not have an equivalent in the social field.

291

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Beyond Essentialism and Constructivism

Commentary on Paper by Cynthia Dyess and Tim Dean

James Hansell PHD

James Hansell

3251 Bluett Road
Ann Arbor, MI 48105
jhansell@umich.edu

James Hansell   Psychoanalyst in private practice in Ann Arbor, Michigan. He is on the faculty at Michigan Psychoanalytic Institute, and Senior Staff at the University of Michigan Psychological Clinic.

The author comments on Dean and Dyess's “Gender: The Impossibility of Meaning.” They provide a valuable critique, based on Lacan's work, of postmodern gender theories. However, Dean and Dyess's argument for the “impossibility of meaning” in regard to gender may erect a new false dichotomy (gender as endless possibility versus gender as total impossibility) in place of the problematic dichotomy (gender essentialism versus gender constructivism) they so usefully deconstruct.

responseSet.title: The Growing Convergence of Contemporary Psychoanalysis and Jungian Analysis
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Growing Convergence of Contemporary Psychoanalysis and Jungian Analysis

Mario Jacoby PHD

Mario Jacoby

Witellikerstrasse 4
CH-8702 Zollikon
Switzerland
mario.jacoby@bluewin.ch

Mario Jacoby   Lecturer and Training and Supervisory Analyst at the C. G. Jung Institute, Zurich; an analyst in private practice; and author of numerous articles and six books on analytical psychology.

Jung's Most Significant Clinical Contributions

After his Break with Freud in 1913 Jung Discarded Many of the “golden rules” of psychoanalysis. Instead of using the couch in order to render himself “invisible,” he had his patients sit in an armchair facing the analyst. He abandoned the basic psychoanalytic rule of free association and tried to facilitate a dialogue in which the patient's problems and “the manifestations of the unconscious” could be discussed. Instead of listening with free-floating attention, he would spontaneously express his ideas about dreams and other material. He did not restrict himself to interpretation, but allowed himself, as the therapist, to come forth with his own emotional reactions.

What are the reasons to justify these modifications? First, they came out of Jung's growing experience of the mutual influence of analyst and analysand. “By no device can the treatment be anything but the product of mutual influence, in which the whole being of the doctor as well as that of his patient plays its part” (Jung, 1929, p. 71). Jung was a pioneer with this insight, which anticipated such contemporary models of therapy as the interactive field, systems analysis, and so forth.

Furthermore, Jung began to look at “all theories of neurosis and methods of treatment as a dubious affair”—arguing that any method of treatment can be determined only “by the nature of the case” (Jung, 1926, p. 113).

489

His new book, The Secret Life of Politics, will be published in 2000. 148 Mercers RoadLondon N19 4PXEnglandandrew.samuels@virgin.net
responseSet.title: The Erotic Leader
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Erotic Leader

Anderw Samuels D.H.L.

Anderw Samuels

148 Mercers Road
London N19 4PX
England
andrew.samuels@virgin.net

Anderw Samuels   Professor of Analytical Psychology, University of Essex; a Jungian training analyst, lecturer, and political consultant; and the author of The Political Psyche. His new book, The Secret Life of Politics, will be published in 2000.

In a nutshell, my proposal is that the strong collective and individual reactions to the trials and tribulations of President Clinton, leading to his survival in office, show that he has functioned in a good-enough way as an “erotic leader.”

From my point of view, a truly erotic leader is not a leader who simply hops in and out of bed with people. Clinton may actually be an inferior, degenerated, partial version of an erotic leader. But public obsession in many countries with the sex lives of leaders suggests that there is something about the actual, physical, corporeal eroticism of leadership that is worth exploring further. The concern of the media with the sexuality of leading figures in society generally, not just political leaders, suggests that, as in so many cases, mass consciousness has discovered something of interest. I think that the populace, in its obsession with the sexuality of leaders, is on to something important.

I see the good-enough erotic leader as using her or his sexuality to convey to citizens that they are exciting, creative, and autonomous people who can work together cooperatively. Ordinary admiration and warmth are extremely potent parts of psychological evolution and the establishment of self-esteem. Whether the leader communicates, in an almost physically warm way, that the leader admires the people is an interesting and neglected aspect of the political process.

My model for this notion is the work I have done on parenting and on clinical process—work focusing on what I have called erotic playback. In the family, this is the way in which the parent communicates to children of both sexes that they are admirable, physically desirable, and erotically viable creatures. Of course, in a family or in an analysis, I am referring only to incest fantasy and not to the physical enactment of such fantasy (which, by analogy, is what Clinton has done).

277

responseSet.title: Dreaming to Different Beats: Reply to Commentaries
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Dreaming to Different Beats

Reply to Commentaries

Hazel Ipp PHD

Hazel Ipp

183 St. Clair Avenue West
Toronto, Ontario M4V 1P7
Canada

Hazel Ipp  

It is both an honor and a challenge to respond to the commentaries on my clinical material. Each reflects considerable thought, effort, and respect. The different theoretical approaches produced considerable departures in terms of understanding and positioning of the dream material. Although I clearly found myself resonating to those approaches more closely allied with my own, I found each of the commentaries evocative in its own right—stirring new questions and generating fresh thoughts as I reconsidered Barbara along the different lines posited.

As always, working with disembodied clinical material is no easy task. So much of importance is lost in the telling, and those outside the analysis are not privy to the ambience; the to-and-fro over time; the naunces that blossom and take shape in the material and in the interactional field; and, most especially in this example, the extensions of meaning of the dream imagery and affect that are so often a continuation of much that has gone before in the analytic process itself. Given the small window opening into Barbara's world, Adams, Ellman, Fosshage, and Levenson have ably captured certain critical features that contribute to her essence.

The commentators' portrayls of Barbara are wide-ranging—she is, variously, seen as extremely narcissistic, prone to borderline rages, grandiose and with significant ego defects suggestive of a poor prognosis, a highly traumatized woman with an unrealistic sense of her own limitations who leads an indolent life with questionable values, and a woman with a growing sense of expansiveness and possibility as she renegotiates her sense of self and self with others through good use of the analytic process. Although there is some overlap in aspects of the understandings put forth, the spin put on them differs dramatically.

responseSet.title: Afterword: A View from the Outside
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Afterword

A View from the Outside

James Fosshage PHD

James Fosshage

330 West 58th Street, Suite 200
New York, NY 10019
fosshage@psychoanalysis.net

James Fosshage  

For each psychoanalytic model, the motivation posited is fundamental and has a rippling effet throughout the theory and clinical application. Jung's theory is no different. What is different is that Jung was the first to posit a fundamental striving toward realizing the potentialities of the person, what Jung called self-realization and the process of individuation. Although conscious intentions exist, Jung posited that there is an unconscious center of the psyche, called the Self, that regulates and organizes “processes of maturation” (Jacoby). Although Young-Eisendrath refers to these psychological functions as the archetype of the self (not Self), and Sedgwick tends to be more concerned with the sense of self, all of our post-Jungians retain Jung's motivational model and the correspondent notion of unconscious self-regulatory and self-directing or maturational functions. Within psychoanalysis, this self-realization motivational model and view of the self, including self-regulatory functions, most closely correspond with the work of Winnicott and Kohut, and, not surprisingly, they are the two authors the post-Jungians refer to most.

Addressing Jung's major contributions, the post-Jungians listed the concept of Self and the corresponding motivational model; the primacy of affect; the bipersonal, mutual influence field created by analyst and analysand; the concept of archetypes; the required deep involvement of the analyst in the treatment; the analyst's sharing of his or her emotional reactions; the importance of the transference and countertransference; a life-span model; a theory of complexes; and the importance of dreams as a more direct expression of the analysand's psychological situation. It is my view that Jung is not known by psychoanalysts for many of these contributions. What is striking is how fitting many of these ideas are to a contemporary relational view of the analytic relationship. Indeed, Young-Eisendrath argues that “analytical psychology is consonant with postmodernism when its models and methods are understood from a nonessentialist viewpoint that is …not a revision but an extension of Jung's own thinking.

responseSet.title: Does She Do or Does She Don't?: Reply to Commentary
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Does She Do or Does She Don't?

Reply to Commentary

Velleda Ceccoli PHD

Velleda Ceccoli

330 West 58th Street, Suite 504
New York, New York 10019
vceccoli@aol.com

Velleda Ceccoli   Psychoanalyst in private practice in New York City. She is a faculty member at the New York University Medical School and the Karen Horney Institute.

This reply clarifies the ideas originaly presented in “Beyond Milk and the Good Breast: Reconfiguring Psychoanalytic Dyads” (PD 9/5, 1999) in response to Steven Reisner's commentary. It faults Dr. Reisner's reading of Lacan and Kristeva, as well as his use of male-gendered metaphors to transform my clinical material into a different analytic treatment. I conclude that, by arriving at such differing conclusions regarding theory and clinical treatment, he inadvertently proves, and enacts, the basis of my argument: that the gender of the analyst is an important variable that affects psychoanalytic formulations, treatment, and outcome.

responseSet.title: Sex, Lies, and Audiotape: Psychoanalysts Reflect on President Clinton's Impeachment
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Sex, Lies, and Audiotape

Psychoanalysts Reflect on President Clinton's Impeachment

Emanuel Berman PHD and Jay Frankel PHD

Jay Frankel

Haifa 31905, Israel
Emanuel@psy.HAIFA.AC.IL

286 Tillou Road
South Orange, NJ 07079
Drifrankel@aol.com

Jay Frankel   Co-Director, Manhattan Institute for Psychoanalysis; Supervisor, New York University Postdoctoral Program in Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis; and Associate Editor of Psychoanalytic Dialogues.

Are psychoanalysts capable of contributing, through their unique expertise, to the understanding of social and political topics? If so, should they get involved?

The dangers of such adventures far away from the consulting room have become clearer in recent decades. “Applied analysis,” as such nonclinical explorations used to be called, often became synonymous with omniscient reductionistic interpretations that disregarded the unique characteristics of their subject matter, be it literature, film (Berman, 1998), or politics, and turned everybody (national leaders, painters, fictional figures) into patients on our couch. Such imperialistic invasions often aroused the justified wrath of academic experts and of creative artists in the various fields invaded (Berman, 1997).

Should these unhappy results lead us to abandon any such endeavor? Not necessarily. Such a puristic solution may risk throwing the baby out with the bathwater.

In the past, a contrast was often suggested between the safe ground of clinical psychoanalysis, in which analysts can reach definitive understanding of the material, and the shaky ground of applied analysis, in which our efforts are more speculative. Our current perspective does not justify such a dichotomy.

267

<autaff><addr><ln>127 West 79th Street, Suite 3</ln><ln>New York, NY 10024</ln><ln>neilaltman@hotmail.com</ln></addr></autaff></artauth></artinfo>
responseSet.title: Reply to Commentaries
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Reply to Commentaries

Neil Altman PHD

Neil Altman

127 West 79th Street, Suite 3
New York, NY 10024
neilaltman@hotmail.com

Neil Altman  

I am very grateful to Drs. Gump and Cushman for their thoughtful, thought-provoking, and stimulating discussions of my paper.

When I first presented the paper, in Washington, DC, Gump was the discussant. She said, as you have now read, that Mr. A was “enlisting” me in his “struggle with a conflicted sense of self”—between his sense of self as a responsible professional man who could be expected to handle a checking account and his sense of self as a Bronx street tough who couldn't. I realized that, by accepting his initial offer to bring cash, I was communicating to him that I was unreflectively seeing him as the “street kid.”

This was a startling insight. It was one of those appalling and fascinating psychoanalytic moments in which one sees one's own blind spot. I realized that a racist attitude (in the form of low expectations) had been most evident in a place where I wasn't at all aware of it, for all my preoccupation with racism at many points in the treatment.

Where I might differ with Gump is in her comment that Mr. A. was seeking to evoke my “acceptance.” I think what is at issue in this as in all enactments is more complex. I believe that Mr. A was unconsciously seeking my disapproval, anger, and greed, as well as my acceptance and understanding. In my view, he was unconsciously seeking to put on the table between us the most problematic affects and interpersonal entanglements taking shape at the intersection of our psychic worlds, in this particular historical context. How he and I, both of us also seeking love and understanding, would find our way through this particular minefield was the question of the treatment.

We, as psychoanalysts, have been garnering considerable experience in managing such complexities, but we have been slow to apply our knowledge of the subtle and complex workings of the mind to the field of race relations on both individual and social levels. Our failure to make this application is revealing about the anxiety, shame, and guilt that attend the matter of race for us as Americans and as psychoanalysts.

responseSet.title: The Psyche as a Process
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psyche as a Process

Beverley Zabriskie C.S.W.

Beverley Zabriskie

15412 East 74th Street
New York, NY 10021

Beverley Zabriskie   Jungian analyst in New York City, is Faculty, New York C. G. Jung Institute, and C. G. Jung Center of Mexico City, and Assistant Editor, Journal of Analytical Psychology and Journal of Jungian Theory and Practice.

Jung's Most Significant Clinical Contributions

Jung (1947) Perceived the Psyche as a Dynamic Interactive System that attempts to organize itself through “the equilibration of all kinds of opposites” (1947/1954, p. 207). The healthy psyche is an ongoing process between conscious and unconscious as two fields, a term and concept Jung adopted from William James. As two modes of psychic knowing and experiencing that relativize each other, these move along a vector toward individuation, understood as psychic balance. Via the interplay between these psychic complementarities, another term from James, psychic health is achieved through alternating rhythms of integration and disassociation. The practitioner is to enter into, attend, and participate in the self-reflective process by which a patient recognizes and negotiates the rhythms of opposites.

As a young psychiatrist working under Bleuler with an inpatient population, Jung perceived neurotic dissociation and more florid splitting as indications of an ego incapable of holding itself sufficiently intact to metabolize these tensions in the face of outer and inner challenges. This perspective emphasized the disassociative nature of symptoms, which informs much clinical work today.

In Jung's view, although symptoms emerge from unmetabolized residues of past experience, as psychic energy is also prospective (1928), they are also attempts from the unconscious to break through binding constructs of a fixated ego. Seemingly meaningless, each symptom may be unpacked for the meaning of both its contents and dynamic purpose.

Jung also approached the psyche as “a multiplicity within a unity,” based on his theory of complexes.
